_note: schema v3 — readiness-flow (no stages, no streams, tags instead). See $GDRIVE/Shared/ecosystem/STATUS.md for semantics.

tasks.title: Resolve ADR-0001 open questions and move to Accepted
acceptance_criteria: ADR-0001 status updated to Accepted with the three open questions closed in the Changelog
If emergent.py is split, the pure-rule portion lives under living_world/rules/ and no agents/ module imports from rules/ (mechanically checked with grep)
chronicler vs narrator event-budget rule documented in the ADR and reflected in tick_loop orchestration
Cost-delta note added to the ADR showing expected per-tick spend change from dropping Tier-2
tasks.description: ADR-0001 is Proposed as of 2026-04-18. Before it can move to Accepted, the three open questions in its 'Risks and open questions' section must be resolved: (1) second-pass review of agents/emergent.py (352 LOC) and agents/self_update.py (288 LOC) for internal structure; (2) division of labor between chronicler and narrator to avoid duplicate LLM spend on overlapping event context; (3) cost-delta pass for the Tier-2 drop so the net change in per-tick LLM spend is understood rather than assumed.

tasks.title: Enforce agents/ vs rules/ boundary in CI
acceptance_criteria: CI fails if any file under living_world/rules/ contains 'llm.complete'
CI fails if any file under living_world/agents/ has 'from living_world.rules' or 'import living_world.rules'
Invariant is documented in README or CONTRIBUTING so future contributors see it without reading the ADR
tasks.description: ADR-0001 specifies that `grep -r 'llm.complete' living_world/rules/` must return zero hits, forever. Today this is true by inspection but not enforced. Add a CI check (script + workflow hook, or a pytest sentinel) that fails the build if any module under living_world/rules/ calls llm.complete, and that also fails if any module under living_world/agents/ imports from living_world/rules/.

decisions.title: Split statmachine into agents/ (LLM) and rules/ (deterministic mechanics); drop Tier-2 from LLM router
decisions.rationale: Pre-refactor statmachine/ mixed deterministic mechanics with LLM-routed cognition, so the 'does this module cost tokens?' question could only be answered by reading the body. The three-tier LLM budget also paid a complexity tax without clear benefit — most events were either cheap enough to be pure rules or important enough to warrant a full LLM call. Splitting into agents/ (9 modules, all call llm.complete) and rules/ (7 modules, zero LLM calls) makes the boundary mechanically checkable by grep, and dropping Tier-2 collapses the budget to a binary regime that call sites can reason about in one step.
